book.title: Make It Stick
book.author: Peter C. Brown, Henry L. Roediger III, and Mark A. McDaniel

chapters.title: Retrieval Builds Stronger Memory Than Rereading

summaryBullets: Using retrieval practice to strengthen learning more than passive review creates clearer judgment.
Memory grows stronger when people pull knowledge out instead of only taking it in.
This matters because durable learning depends on effortful recall more than on comfort.
A common mistake is confusing recognition during rereading with real mastery.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the material feels familiar but cannot be produced without the page.
A practical starting move is to quiz yourself before rereading, close the notes and recall, and write what you remember first.
keyTakeaways: Using retrieval practice to strengthen learning more than passive review creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.
practice: quiz yourself before rereading
close the notes and recall
write what you remember first
use low stakes retrieval often
